Lenders should watch Cotton closely — at 4.8%, it is the weakest link in the recovery.

inferences: Jute credit grew 19.9% YoY, the fastest in Textiles, followed by Other at 13.3%, Cotton at 4.8%, and Man-Made at 2.7%.
Other Textiles and Man-Made both shifted from strengthening to active status, signaling momentum moderation.
All four segments are growing, but the 17.2pp spread shows Jute and Other are pulling away from Cotton and Man-Made.

gaps.title: Pharma share dips to 33.5% — Fertiliser stable at 10.2%
gaps.body: Within Chemicals, Others holds 44.1%, Drugs and Pharmaceuticals 33.5%, Petro Chemicals 12.2%, and Fertiliser 10.2%. Drugs and Pharmaceuticals declined from 33.9% → 33.5%, shifting from strengthening to active. Fertiliser rose from 9.9% → 10.2%, moving from weakening to active. The spread narrowed from 34.3pp to 33.8pp.
gaps.implication: Pharma's share loss and status shift to active suggests its growth premium is fading — lenders are no longer concentrating exposure there. Fertiliser's stabilisation at 10.2% indicates the worst of the contraction may be over, but it remains the smallest segment. With all four entities now active, Chemicals credit is broadening rather than concentrating.

inferences: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals lost 0.4pp share to 33.5%, reversing its prior strengthening trend.
Fertiliser gained 0.3pp to 10.2%, stabilising after prior weakness.
All four segments are now active — no entity is strengthening or weakening, signalling balanced growth across Chemicals.

gaps.title: Pharma surges to 20.6% YoY — Fertiliser contracts -0.3%
gaps.body: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% year-on-year, Petro Chemicals 19.4%, and Others 16.4%. Fertiliser contracted -0.3%. Drugs and Pharmaceuticals accelerated from 17.9% → 20.6%, shifting from active to strengthening. Others accelerated from 14.9% → 16.4%, moving from active to strengthening. Fertiliser improved from -4.5% → -0.3%, but remains in decline. The spread narrowed from 32.3pp to 20.9pp.
gaps.implication: Pharma's 20.6% growth and strengthening status confirm robust healthcare demand — despite losing share, absolute credit expansion is accelerating. Fertiliser's near-zero contraction signals the structural stress is easing, likely on policy support or input cost relief. The narrowing spread and two strengthening entities point to broad-based momentum across Chemicals, with Pharma leading the charge.

highlight: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels

inferences: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% YoY, a 2.7pp acceleration, flipping to strengthening status.
Fertiliser's contraction moderated sharply from -4.5% to -0.3%, nearing stabilisation.
The 20.9pp spread is the narrowest in this window, driven by Fertiliser's recovery and broad-based acceleration across Pharma, Petro, and Others.

gaps.title: Edible Oils 33.0% YoY leads Food Processing; Sugar turns negative at -0.4%
gaps.body: Edible Oils and Vanaspati grew 33.0% year-on-year, Others 13.6%, Sugar -0.4%, and Tea -3.1%. Sugar shifted from active to declining at -0.4%; Tea moved from strengthening to declining at -3.1%. Edible Oils accelerated from 25.3% to 33.0%. The spread is 36.1 percentage points.
gaps.implication: Edible Oils is the clear growth engine within Food Processing, driven by import substitution, rising consumption, and capacity expansion — lenders should prioritise exposure here. Sugar's turn to contraction signals sector stress — weak prices, inventory buildup, or policy uncertainty. Tea's shift to declining confirms the prior period's recovery was short-lived — avoid new commitments until growth stabilises.

inferences: Edible Oils surged from 25.3% to 33.0% YoY and shifted to strengthening — the fastest growth within Food Processing and a 7.7-point acceleration.
Sugar turned negative at -0.4%, shifting from active to declining — the first contraction in this dataset.
Tea fell from 0.8% to -3.1% and shifted to declining — a 3.9-point deceleration and the weakest reading among Food Processing sub-segments.

gaps.title: Ports 74.0% YoY surges; Railways -44.1%, Airports -26.0% in sharp contraction
gaps.body: Ports grew 74.0% year-on-year, Power 21.5%, and Other Infrastructure 1.4%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecommunications -11.9%. Ports accelerated from 48.7% to 74.0% and shifted to strengthening. Railways deepened from -49.8% to -44.1% but remains in decline. The spread is 118.1 percentage points — the widest in this domain.
gaps.implication: Ports is the standout growth driver within Infrastructure, likely on trade volume recovery, capacity expansion, and logistics capex — lenders should prioritise exposure. Railways and Airports remain in sharp contraction — project delays, refinancing stress, or lender exits — avoid new commitments until growth stabilises. The 118.1-point spread confirms extreme polarisation: Power and Ports are surging while transport segments collapse.

inferences: Ports surged from 48.7% to 74.0% YoY — a 25.3-point acceleration and the fastest growth within Infrastructure.
Railways contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecom -11.9% — three of seven segments are declining, unchanged from the prior period.
The spread widened from 98.5 to 118.1 percentage points — the largest divergence in this dataset, signaling extreme polarisation within Infrastructure credit.
